To see why multiplying the conditional survival factors estimates marginal
survival, write the ordered distinct exit times as
$y_1 < y_2 < \cdots < y_m$.

The Kaplan-Meier construction assumes that the conditional probability of
surviving between consecutive exit times is 1.
For consecutive exit times $y_{j-1}$ and $y_j$, this means:

$$
\Pr(Y \geq y_j \mid Y > y_{j-1}) = 1.
$$

Then:

:::{#thm-km-gaps}

If $\Pr(Y \geq y_j \mid Y > y_{j-1}) = 1$, then:

$$\Pr(Y \geq y_j) = \Pr(Y > y_{j-1})$$
:::

:::{.proof}

$$
\ba
\Pr(Y \geq y_j)
\\
&= \Pr(Y \geq y_j, Y > y_{j-1})
\\
&= \Pr(Y \geq y_j \mid Y > y_{j-1}) \Pr(Y > y_{j-1})
\\
&= 1 \Pr(Y > y_{j-1})
\\
&= \Pr(Y > y_{j-1})
\ea
$$
:::

Thus all the between-exit-time factors drop out, and the marginal survival
through an exit time $y_j$ can be written recursively as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr(Y > y_j)
&= \Pr(Y > y_j \mid Y \geq y_j)\Pr(Y \geq y_j)\\
&= \Pr(Y > y_j \mid Y \geq y_j)\Pr(Y > y_{j-1})\\
&= \kappa(y_j)\Pr(Y > y_{j-1}).
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying this recursion back to the first exit time gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\Pr}(Y > t)
&= \hat{S}_{KM}(t)\\
&= \prod_{k:\, y_k \leq t} \hat{\Pr}(Y > y_k \mid Y \geq y_k)\\
&= \prod_{k:\, y_k \leq t} \hat{\kappa}(y_k)\\
&= \prod_{k:\, y_k \leq t} \frac{r(y_k) - d(y_k)}{r(y_k)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

For any time $t$ between two consecutive exit times, the estimate stays
constant, because the only additional conditional survival factors are 1.
